{28 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA g-5g14-2329
(916) 322-5660 0 Fax (916) 322-0886
March 4,2008
Jack L. White
Office of the City Attorney
200 S. Anaheim Blvd, Suite 356
Anaheinr, Califomia 52805
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our F-ile No. A-08-016
Dear Mr. White
This letter rcsponds to your request for advice of behalf of Anaheim City
Councilmcrnher Lucille,Kring regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the political
Refrrrrn Act (the "Act")' and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice.
(ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to obligarions
arising ttnder the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-
interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTONS
1. Does Councilmember Kring have a potential conflict of interest that would
prohibit her from participating in a governmental decision concerning adoption of the
SOAR Initiative measure and cancellation of the previously schedul.a rp"iiut election?
2. If it is determined that Councilmember Kring has a potential conflict of
interest that would otherwise prohibit her from participating ina governmental decision
concerning adoption of the SOAR Initiative measllre and cancellalion of the previously
scheduled special election, wor-rld the "public generally" exception nevertheless allow her
to participate?
' The Political Re'f<rrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g 1000 through 91014. All
statutoryreferencesaretotheGovernntentCode.unlessotherwiseindicated.'IheregulatioisrlftheFair
Political Practices Commission are c0ntained in Sections lllll0 through ltiggT of Title 2 of the califirrnia
Code of Regulations. All legulat<lry ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitirrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. 4-08-016
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
l' [f Councilmember Kring can- rebut the presumption of materiality with respect to
her leased property, and she determines that the decision will not have a reaslnably
foreseeable financial effect on her business, as outlined below, she will not have a conflict of
interest in participating in the decision.
2' You have not presented enough facts for us to determine if the "public generally,'
exception would apply, but given the limited facts you have presented it is ixtremely
unlikely.
FACTS
The City of Anaheim (the "City") is-a charter city consisting of approximately 50
square miles in northern orange county. The city has a current population of
approximately 343,000. There are approximately 2,857 retail businesses licensed within
the City of Anaheim.
Councilmember Lucille Kring was first elected to the Anaheim City Council (the
"Council") in November l99g and served one four-year term, which expired in
November 2002. She was not a member of the Council between November 2OOZ and,
November 2006. She was re-elected to the Council in Novemb er 2Oo6where she
currently serves.
The Anaheim Garden Walk (the "Garden Walk") Project is an approximately 29.1
acre mixed use project bordered by Katella Avenue, ruaibor Boulevard, tiirr"y way, and
clementine Street. The full project at build out will be composed of up to 569,750 square
f.:!:{ specialty retail, restauiants, and entertainment uses, including: movie theaters;
1,628 hotel rooms - including up to 500 vacation ownership unitsizzs,g27 square feet
of hotel accessory uses; a transportation center; and 4,800 off-rt.""iparkinj splces. The
Garden walk project will be built in multiple phases. The first phase, whiJh in.tua",
approximately 439,600 square feet of retail, reitaurants, and entertainment uses, is
clllrently under construction with an anticipated opening date in or around Spring 200g.
The Anaheim Resort is the only area of the City designated by the General plan
for commercial recreation land uses. This General ptan lanO use designation is'
implemented by three specific plans: the Disneyland Resort Specific-plan, the Anaheim
Resort Specific PIan, and the Hotel Circle specific Plan. The Disneyland Resort specific
Plan applies to 490 acres within The Anaheim Resort and includes the Disner*o theme
parks and Downtown Disney. The Anaheim Resort Specific plan provides for the
development of approximately 582 acres within The Anaheim Resort with hotels, motels,
convention and conference facilities, restaurants, retail shops, and entertainment
facilities. The Hotel Circle specific Plan applies to only 6-8 acres within the Anaheim
Resort' The Garden walk property is located within the Disneyland Resort Specific plan
area.


File No. 4-08-016
Page No. 3
On September 9, 2006, before her current term of service on the city council,
Councilmember Kring signed a nonbinding letter of intent with the Garden Walk
indicating her desire and intent to subsequently enter into a l0-year lease of
approximately 2,000 sqllare feet of commercial retail space in the Garden Walk for
operation of a wine bar and enumerating proposed terms of the potential lease. In August
2006 Councilmember Kring retained the services of an architeci to design the interior
space in the proposed wine bar. However, Councilmember Kring witt*ieta authorization
for the architect to commence design work and incur expenses at that time. Due to tenant
commitments and changes in tenancies within the Garden Walk, the project manager
subsequently advised Councilmember Kring that the proposed site of the wine bar would
need to be relocated to a different space within the Garden Walk. A new nonbinding
letter of intent was executed for the revised location in Septemb er 2007. The size of the
leased space will be in the range of 2,000-2,800 square feet. the architect was not
authorized by Councilmember Kring to begin *ork o, designing the space and incurring
expenses until October 2007. In February 2007, CouncilmemUei fring filed papers ro
form a California limited liability company for the ftrture business ond'p."..rue and
protect the name of the wine bar as ,.pop the Cork, LLC.,,
Councilmember Kring has now entered into a binding lease agreement to operate
the wine bar at a site within the Garden Walk development. The wine bar will sell wine
by the glass and bottle, wine and gourmet gift baskets, food items, and other accessory
items as yet to be determined; a portion ofihe leased premises will be available for
meetings and catered events. for small groups. Since incorporating "pop the Cork, LLC,,,
she has invested more than $2,000 in the business in prepaiing to -op", ihe wine bar. The
Garden Walk development, including Councilmembei rring'i wini bar business, is
located within the boundaries of the Anaheim Resort.
On March 19,2007, Save Our Anaheim Resort Area ("SOAR") filed a notice of
intent to circulate an initiative petition concerning a measure (the SOAR Initiative
measure) to amend various provisions of the Anaheim General plan, Anaheim Resort
Specific Plan, Disneyland Resort Specific Plan, and Horel Circle Specific plan
(collectively, the "Anaheim Resort area" or the "specific plans") to timit the land uses
permitted or conditionally permitted within the Anaheim Resort Area to theme parks,
hotels, motels, vacation ownership resorts, convention and conference facilities,
restaurants, retail and entenainment facilities, and other visitor-serving uses, as provided
for in the specific plans in effect as of March lg,2007 . No residentiaiuses of property
would be permitted or conditionally permitted in the Anaheim Resort area excJpt that
residential uses integrated into full-service hotels having at least 300 rooms woirld be
conditionally permitted in limited areas of the Anaheim-Resort Specific plan area as
provided by and in accordance with the Anaheim Resort Resideniial overlay in effect on
March 19,2007, and such Llses as permitted by and in accordance with the Mobile Home
Park Overlay in effect as of March 19,2007. The initiative measure also provides that the
boundaries of each of the specific plans and the Anaheim Reson area would be those
boundaries in effect on March lg,2Oo7. The measure also provides that the permitted
and conditionally permitted land uses and boundaries of the Anaheim Resort area,


File No. ,4-08-016
Page No.4
Anaheim Resort Specific Plan, and Anaheim Resort Residential Overlay shall not be
amended' nor shall other residential uses be incorporated into the Commercial-Recreation
land use designation, except by: ( I ) completion oi an environmental impact report; (2)
completion of a long-term economic impact analysis; (3) approval of thl city council; and
(4) approval by a majority of the voters at a reguiarty scheauted municipal eiection.
The SOAR [nitiative would apply only to the properties located in the Anaheim
Resort and does not change the permitted use of thos; properties, but keeps the permitted
use of property the same as those which were permittea aJ of March tg, iool . It imposes
additional requirements, including a vote of thi electorate, to approve any change to
those permitted uses. 
t 
J -'--
The City currently contains 57,002 properties of which 326 arelocated in the
Anaheim Resort- The City currently contains 1,857 retail businesses of which 179 are
located in the Anaheim Resort.
on october L0,2007, the initiative petition for the soAR Initiative measure was
certified by the elections official as containing valid signatures of not less than fifteen
percent of the qualified electors of the City. Thereforq in accordance with the
requirements of Section 9214 of the Elections Code, the city council must either adopt
the SOAR Initiative measure or order a special election at which the measure shall be
submitted to a vote of the electorate of the City.
On October 23,2007,the Anaheim City Council adopted a resolution scheduling
the SOAR Initiative measure for consideration at a special election to be held on June 3,
2008. The cost of the special election is estimated to be approximately $250,000.
Due to the cost of such an election as well as other considerations,
Councilmember Kring, and possibly other members of the city council, desire to consider
adoption of the SOAR Initiative measure and cancellation of ihe special election. Under
the provisions of the Elections Code, the last day on which the city council could adopt
the SoAR Initiative and cancel the special election is March tz. inelart.egJa.ty
scheduled city council meeting is on March 4, Z0Og.
PorrN rrar Hf,t li"l. n rr*r,
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensllre that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported th;." (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from-making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc. 
-u gou.*rnental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.


File No. A-08-016
Page No. 5
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation rszootul.) The general
ntle, however' is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.
srrps 1&2: Is couNcrLMEMBER KnrNc A purlrc orrrcr.rr, MAKTNG,
PaRrtcparING rN Mlt<rNGn oR INFLUENCTNG A GovERNMENTAL DncIstoN?
As a member of the Anaheim City Council, Councilmember Kring is a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, she may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise.use her official position tolnfluence any decisions thai will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of heieconomic interests.
councilmember Kring will be called upon to considei whether the city should approve or
disapprove of adopting the SoAR Initiative. Therefore, she will be making, parricipating
in making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a govemmental decision.
srnp 3: Dors CouNcu,uEMBER Knmc Havn A porENTrALLy Drsquar,rrvmc
EcoNonrrc Ixrnnnsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect' distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
invesrment of $2,000 or more (section sito:(a); reg. lg703.l(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,-or holds any-position of
managemenr (section 87103(d); reg. 1 g703.1(b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 97r03(b); reg. rg703.z);
o An economic interest in any source of income, incruding promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to thJdecision (section g7103(c);
reg. 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(J); ,"glTa,7'u.+l:
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "per.sonal financial effects" rule
(section 87103; reg. 18703.5).


File No. 4-08-016
Page No.6
Section 82033 defines real property as: ..... any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an inteiest in real propefty located in the
jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public ofri.iut ...,, Because
Councilmember Kring has entered into a 1O-year lease of the property in the Garden
Walk, and that lease is p.resumably valued at $2,000 or more, she has an economic
interest in real property.l
Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operared for profit, including but nor limited to a propiietoiship, partnership, fir-,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association.,'
In order for a business entity to be considered an economic interest, an official
must have an investment of $2,000 or more or be a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or hold any position of management. In our previous letter, (White Advice
Letter, No. 4-07-035) we concluded that under the facts presented at that time,
"Councilmember Kring is not clllTently operating, nor does she have any investment in or
hold any position with, the wine bar." Accordinlly, we found that..Councilmember
Kring [did] not have an economic interest in a business entity.,,
Since that time you have now indicated that Councilmember Kring has
incorporated and is an officer of the corporation. Furthermore, she has leased a location
to operate her business and has hired an architect to design the space. Accordingly, she
has a position in the corporation and, presumably, an inv"estment of more than g2,000.
However, because the definition of business entity states that the organization must be
"operated" for profit, and you have indicated that the business has y-et to open, the
question remains as to whether the entity, as this point, meets the definition of a business
entity.
Section 81003 requires that the Act "should be liberally construed to accomplish
its purposes." For that.reasol we interpret the phrase "any organ izationor enterprise
operated for profit" to include any existing tegil entity that has been formed to operate as
a business, whether it has made its first .ui. oi not. clearly, ,.pop the cork, LLC,, meets
this criterion and is, therefore, a business entity under the ict.
Srrp 4: Is Tun EcoNol'ttc lNrrnrsr DrRrcrly on lNnrnrcrr,y lNvorvBo IN THE
GovBnNtTENTAL DBctsroN?
"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) For gor.*-.ntal decisions that affect
real property interests, the standards set forth in regulition 18704.Zapply. lRegulation
: See regulation I 8729(b) for valuing leasehold interests.


File No. 4-08-016
Page No. 7
18704(a)(2).) For governmental decisions that affect business entities, the standards set
forth in regulation 18704.1 apply. (Regulation lg70a(aXl).)
Real Prooertv:
Regulation 18704.2(a) stares, in pertinenr part:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of
the following apply:
"(l) T!9 real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the properry [rhar] is
the subject of the governmental decision.,,
Because Councilmember Kring's property is located within the boundaries of the
property that is the subject of the governmental d'ecision, her property is directly
involved. If the real property is directly involved in the governmental decisions, the
materiality standard set forth in Regulation 1g705.2(a) applies. (Regulation
18704'2(d)(1).) (See Step 5 below for discussion of the applicabilir; of Regulation
18705.2.)
Business Entitv:
Regulation I 8704. l(a) states:
"(a) A persor' including business entities, sources of income, and sources
of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency *t "n
that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or theofficial's agency. A persJn is the sub]ect
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuancl, renewar, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlemenito, or
contract with, the subject person."
Because Councilmember Kring's business is not initiating the proceeding and is
neither a named party in or the subject of the proceeding involviig the S9AR Initiative
measure, her business is indirectly involved.
For indirectly involved business entities, the materiality standards set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c) apply. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) (See Step s below for discussion
of the applicability of Regulation 19705. 1.)


File No. ,{-08-016
Page No. 8
SrBp 5: MarnRrarrry STANDARD
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 1 8700(a).)
Real Prooertv:
For real property leaseholds directly involved in a governmental decision, the
materiality standards in Regulation 18705.2(aX2) provide:
"(a) Directly involved real property.
{...}
"(2) Real property, leaseholds. The financial effect of a
governmental decision on the real property in which an official has a
leasehold interest is presumed to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that thl governmental
decision will have any effect on any of the following:
(A) The termination date of the lease;
(B)The amount of rent paid by the lessee for the leased real property,
either positively or negatively;
(C) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real property, either
positively or negatively;
(D) The legally allowable use or the current use of the real property
by the lessee; or
(E) The use or enjoyment of the leased real property by the lessee.
Under the facts you have provided, there do not appear to be any factors involved in
the decision that would have any impact on the lease premises as far as the terms indicated in
(A-E) above, and Councilmember Kring's may, therelore, be able to rebut the presumption
that the decision will have a material effecl on her leased property. However, our analysis is
limited to the facts presented. There may be other factors'that pr;vide a more complete
overview of the potential impact of the decision on Councilmember Kring's property, and the
ultimate decision as to whether the presumption can be rebutted is for heito ,u[".
Business Entitv:
For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business- For relatively small businesses (those with net incoire of less than


File No. A-08-016
Page No. 9
$500,000 for the most recent fiscal year)3 the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(cXa) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( l)-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material
if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The.governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
Srnp 6: Rresoxlnly FoRESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether rhe
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good
faith effort to assess the financial effects of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective method of valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-l L3 MoockAdvice
Letter, No. A-01-140; o'Harra Advice Letter, No. 4-00-174.) This effort may require
that additional information regarding the potential financial effect of the decision be
gathered and evaluated. A public official is not required to, but may chose to utilize the
services of a professional to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for
conflict of interest purposes. (Hensley, supra.) The Commission does not act as a finder
of fact in making this determinarion. (ln re oglesby (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 71.)
In light of the guidance provided above, Councilmember Kring must determine if,
at the time tlrc decision is made, a measure that prohibits residential uses within the
Anaheim Resort District (unless approved by the electorate), will reasonably foreseeably
result in an increase or decrease in her business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more. If it will not, and if she can rebut the presumption of materiality
with respect to her leased property, she will not have a conflict of interest in participating in
the decision.
I As a new business, this standard applies.


File No. 4-08-016
Page No. l0
The answer to this question. however, depends on factors that we do not have for
examination before us. Ultimately, the governmental decision, as we understand it, could
lead to either more residential consumers in the area or more business/recreational
consumers, and this factor may have an influence in determining the financial effect on
Councilmember Kring's business. Whether it does or not depends on the type of business
plan or marketing strategy Councilmember Kring envisions for her business. If one of her
goals, for example, is to hold high-end functions for business travelers, it would certainly
seem that she would benefit more from the building of high-end hotels than housing, and she
may surpass the $20,000 materiality threshold mentioned above.
In the end, the call as to whether is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will meet the materiality standard for her business must be made by Councilmember
Kring, as she is the one who possesses the knowledge necessary for this determination to be
made.
Srrp 7: Pusllc GnNpn.lr,r,y
If Councilmember Kring determines that she has a conflict of interest
participating in any of the governmental decision, the conflict will not be disqualifying if
she can establish that the decision will affect her economic interest in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the "public
generally." This is known, appropriately, as the "public generally" exception. In order
for the exception to apply, the govemmental decision must affect a "significant segment"
of the population. For business entities, this is defined as either 2,000 businesses or 25
percent of the businesses in the City ((Regulation 18707.1(b)(l)(C)) and for properry
owner, this is defined as 5,000 property owners or l0 percent of all property owners
(Regulation 18707.1(bXlXBXi, ii)), and the governmental decision must financially
affect Councilmember Kring's business and property interest in "substantially the same
manner" as it will affect the significant segment identified. (Regulation 18707.1(bX2).)
Given the limited facts you have supplied, (that "the City currently contains
57,002 properties of which 326 are located in the Anaheim Resort," and "2,857 retail
businesses of which 179 are located in the Anaheim Resort"), its does not appear that the
decision will affect enough businesses or property owners to meet the required
"significant segment" threshold. Additionally, although you have indicated that the
decision "would affect all of the properties within the Anaheim Resort in substantially the
same manner," it must affect all those properties or businesses in the significant segment
within the entire jurisdiction, and not just those within the Anaheim Resort, in
substantially the same manner.
Accordingly, under the facts before us at this time, the public generally exception
will not apply.
Srnp 8: Lrcelly Rngurnru PlnrrcrpluoN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "legally required
participation" exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we have not provided an
analysis involving this step.


File No. A-08-016
Page No. l1
If you have other qllestions on this matter, please contact me at (g16) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: William J
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Under the facts you have provided, there do not appear to be any factors involved in
the decision that would have any impact on the lease premises as far as the terms indicated in
(A-E) above, and Councilmember Kring's may, therelore, be able to rebut the presumption
that the decision will have a material effecl on her leased property. However, our analysis is
limited to the facts presented. There may be other factors'that pr;vide a more complete
overview of the potential impact of the decision on Councilmember Kring's property, and the
ultimate decision as to whether the presumption can be rebutted is for heito ,u[".
Business Entitv:
For business entities not directly involved in the governmental decision,
Regulation 18705.1 provides for materiality standards that vary according to the financial
size of the business- For relatively small businesses (those with net incoire of less than
$500,000 for the most recent fiscal year)3 the materiality standards set forth in Regulation
18705.1(cXa) apply:
"(4) If the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( l)-(3), the
financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material
if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The.governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
Srnp 6: Rresoxlnly FoRESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) Whether rhe
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good
faith effort to assess the financial effects of the decision by using some reasonable and
objective method of valuation. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-l L3 MoockAdvice
Letter, No. A-01-140; o'Harra Advice Letter, No. 4-00-174.) This effort may require
that additional information regarding the potential financial effect of the decision be
gathered and evaluated. A public official is not required to, but may chose to utilize the
services of a professional to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for
conflict of interest purposes. (Hensley, supra.) The Commission does not act as a finder
of fact in making this determinarion. (ln re oglesby (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 71.)
In light of the guidance provided above, Councilmember Kring must determine if,
at the time tlrc decision is made, a measure that prohibits residential uses within the
Anaheim Resort District (unless approved by the electorate), will reasonably foreseeably
result in an increase or decrease in her business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more. If it will not, and if she can rebut the presumption of materiality
with respect to her leased property, she will not have a conflict of interest in participating in
the decision.
I As a new business, this standard applies.


Page No. l0
The answer to this question. however, depends on factors that we do not have for
examination before us. Ultimately, the governmental decision, as we understand it, could
lead to either more residential consumers in the area or more business/recreational
consumers, and this factor may have an influence in determining the financial effect on
Councilmember Kring's business. Whether it does or not depends on the type of business
plan or marketing strategy Councilmember Kring envisions for her business. If one of her
goals, for example, is to hold high-end functions for business travelers, it would certainly
seem that she would benefit more from the building of high-end hotels than housing, and she
may surpass the $20,000 materiality threshold mentioned above.
In the end, the call as to whether is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will meet the materiality standard for her business must be made by Councilmember
Kring, as she is the one who possesses the knowledge necessary for this determination to be
made.
Srrp 7: Pusllc GnNpn.lr,r,y
If Councilmember Kring determines that she has a conflict of interest
participating in any of the governmental decision, the conflict will not be disqualifying if
she can establish that the decision will affect her economic interest in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the "public
generally." This is known, appropriately, as the "public generally" exception. In order
for the exception to apply, the govemmental decision must affect a "significant segment"
of the population. For business entities, this is defined as either 2,000 businesses or 25
percent of the businesses in the City ((Regulation 18707.1(b)(l)(C)) and for properry
owner, this is defined as 5,000 property owners or l0 percent of all property owners
(Regulation 18707.1(bXlXBXi, ii)), and the governmental decision must financially
affect Councilmember Kring's business and property interest in "substantially the same
manner" as it will affect the significant segment identified. (Regulation 18707.1(bX2).)
Given the limited facts you have supplied, (that "the City currently contains
57,002 properties of which 326 are located in the Anaheim Resort," and "2,857 retail
businesses of which 179 are located in the Anaheim Resort"), its does not appear that the
decision will affect enough businesses or property owners to meet the required
"significant segment" threshold. Additionally, although you have indicated that the
decision "would affect all of the properties within the Anaheim Resort in substantially the
same manner," it must affect all those properties or businesses in the significant segment
within the entire jurisdiction, and not just those within the Anaheim Resort, in
substantially the same manner.
Accordingly, under the facts before us at this time, the public generally exception
will not apply.
Srnp 8: Lrcelly Rngurnru PlnrrcrpluoN
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "legally required
participation" exception would be applicable here. Accordingly, we have not provided an
sections.conclusion:
l' [f Councilmember Kring can- rebut the presumption of materiality with respect to
her leased property, and she determines that the decision will not have a reaslnably
foreseeable financial effect on her business, as outlined below, she will not have a conflict of
interest in participating in the decision.
2' You have not presented enough facts for us to determine if the "public generally,'
exception would apply, but given the limited facts you have presented it is ixtremely
unlikely.

The City of Anaheim (the "City") is-a charter city consisting of approximately 50
square miles in northern orange county. The city has a current population of
approximately 343,000. There are approximately 2,857 retail businesses licensed within
the City of Anaheim.
Councilmember Lucille Kring was first elected to the Anaheim City Council (the
"Council") in November l99g and served one four-year term, which expired in
November 2002. She was not a member of the Council between November 2OOZ and,
November 2006. She was re-elected to the Council in Novemb er 2Oo6where she
currently serves.
The Anaheim Garden Walk (the "Garden Walk") Project is an approximately 29.1
acre mixed use project bordered by Katella Avenue, ruaibor Boulevard, tiirr"y way, and
clementine Street. The full project at build out will be composed of up to 569,750 square
f.:!:{ specialty retail, restauiants, and entertainment uses, including: movie theaters;
1,628 hotel rooms - including up to 500 vacation ownership unitsizzs,g27 square feet
of hotel accessory uses; a transportation center; and 4,800 off-rt.""iparkinj splces. The
Garden walk project will be built in multiple phases. The first phase, whiJh in.tua",
approximately 439,600 square feet of retail, reitaurants, and entertainment uses, is
clllrently under construction with an anticipated opening date in or around Spring 200g.
The Anaheim Resort is the only area of the City designated by the General plan
for commercial recreation land uses. This General ptan lanO use designation is'
implemented by three specific plans: the Disneyland Resort Specific-plan, the Anaheim
Resort Specific PIan, and the Hotel Circle specific Plan. The Disneyland Resort specific
Plan applies to 490 acres within The Anaheim Resort and includes the Disner*o theme
parks and Downtown Disney. The Anaheim Resort Specific plan provides for the
development of approximately 582 acres within The Anaheim Resort with hotels, motels,
convention and conference facilities, restaurants, retail shops, and entertainment
facilities. The Hotel Circle specific Plan applies to only 6-8 acres within the Anaheim
Resort' The Garden walk property is located within the Disneyland Resort Specific plan
area.
On September 9, 2006, before her current term of service on the city council,
Councilmember Kring signed a nonbinding letter of intent with the Garden Walk
indicating her desire and intent to subsequently enter into a l0-year lease of
approximately 2,000 sqllare feet of commercial retail space in the Garden Walk for
operation of a wine bar and enumerating proposed terms of the potential lease. In August
2006 Councilmember Kring retained the services of an architeci to design the interior
space in the proposed wine bar. However, Councilmember Kring witt*ieta authorization
for the architect to commence design work and incur expenses at that time. Due to tenant
commitments and changes in tenancies within the Garden Walk, the project manager
subsequently advised Councilmember Kring that the proposed site of the wine bar would
need to be relocated to a different space within the Garden Walk. A new nonbinding
letter of intent was executed for the revised location in Septemb er 2007. The size of the
leased space will be in the range of 2,000-2,800 square feet. the architect was not
authorized by Councilmember Kring to begin *ork o, designing the space and incurring
expenses until October 2007. In February 2007, CouncilmemUei fring filed papers ro
form a California limited liability company for the ftrture business ond'p."..rue and
protect the name of the wine bar as ,.pop the Cork, LLC.,,
Councilmember Kring has now entered into a binding lease agreement to operate
the wine bar at a site within the Garden Walk development. The wine bar will sell wine
by the glass and bottle, wine and gourmet gift baskets, food items, and other accessory
items as yet to be determined; a portion ofihe leased premises will be available for
meetings and catered events. for small groups. Since incorporating "pop the Cork, LLC,,,
she has invested more than $2,000 in the business in prepaiing to -op", ihe wine bar. The
Garden Walk development, including Councilmembei rring'i wini bar business, is
located within the boundaries of the Anaheim Resort.
On March 19,2007, Save Our Anaheim Resort Area ("SOAR") filed a notice of
intent to circulate an initiative petition concerning a measure (the SOAR Initiative
measure) to amend various provisions of the Anaheim General plan, Anaheim Resort
Specific Plan, Disneyland Resort Specific Plan, and Horel Circle Specific plan
(collectively, the "Anaheim Resort area" or the "specific plans") to timit the land uses
permitted or conditionally permitted within the Anaheim Resort Area to theme parks,
hotels, motels, vacation ownership resorts, convention and conference facilities,
restaurants, retail and entenainment facilities, and other visitor-serving uses, as provided
for in the specific plans in effect as of March lg,2007 . No residentiaiuses of property
would be permitted or conditionally permitted in the Anaheim Resort area excJpt that
residential uses integrated into full-service hotels having at least 300 rooms woirld be
conditionally permitted in limited areas of the Anaheim-Resort Specific plan area as
provided by and in accordance with the Anaheim Resort Resideniial overlay in effect on
March 19,2007, and such Llses as permitted by and in accordance with the Mobile Home
Park Overlay in effect as of March 19,2007. The initiative measure also provides that the
boundaries of each of the specific plans and the Anaheim Reson area would be those
boundaries in effect on March lg,2Oo7. The measure also provides that the permitted
and conditionally permitted land uses and boundaries of the Anaheim Resort area,


File No. ,4-08-016
Anaheim Resort Specific Plan, and Anaheim Resort Residential Overlay shall not be
amended' nor shall other residential uses be incorporated into the Commercial-Recreation
land use designation, except by: ( I ) completion oi an environmental impact report; (2)
completion of a long-term economic impact analysis; (3) approval of thl city council; and
(4) approval by a majority of the voters at a reguiarty scheauted municipal eiection.
The SOAR [nitiative would apply only to the properties located in the Anaheim
Resort and does not change the permitted use of thos; properties, but keeps the permitted
use of property the same as those which were permittea aJ of March tg, iool . It imposes
additional requirements, including a vote of thi electorate, to approve any change to
those permitted uses. 
t 
J -'--
The City currently contains 57,002 properties of which 326 arelocated in the
Anaheim Resort- The City currently contains 1,857 retail businesses of which 179 are
located in the Anaheim Resort.
on october L0,2007, the initiative petition for the soAR Initiative measure was
certified by the elections official as containing valid signatures of not less than fifteen
percent of the qualified electors of the City. Thereforq in accordance with the
requirements of Section 9214 of the Elections Code, the city council must either adopt
the SOAR Initiative measure or order a special election at which the measure shall be
submitted to a vote of the electorate of the City.
On October 23,2007,the Anaheim City Council adopted a resolution scheduling
the SOAR Initiative measure for consideration at a special election to be held on June 3,
2008. The cost of the special election is estimated to be approximately $250,000.
Due to the cost of such an election as well as other considerations,
Councilmember Kring, and possibly other members of the city council, desire to consider
adoption of the SOAR Initiative measure and cancellation of ihe special election. Under
the provisions of the Elections Code, the last day on which the city council could adopt
the SoAR Initiative and cancel the special election is March tz. inelart.egJa.ty
scheduled city council meeting is on March 4, Z0Og.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensllre that public officials will ..perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported th;." (Section g1001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from-making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influenc. 
-u gou.*rnental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation rszootul.) The general
ntle, however' is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one
or more of his or her financial interests.

PaRrtcparING rN Mlt<rNGn oR INFLUENCTNG A GovERNMENTAL DncIstoN?
As a member of the Anaheim City Council, Councilmember Kring is a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, she may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise.use her official position tolnfluence any decisions thai will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of heieconomic interests.
councilmember Kring will be called upon to considei whether the city should approve or
disapprove of adopting the SoAR Initiative. Therefore, she will be making, parricipating
in making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a govemmental decision.
srnp 3: Dors CouNcu,uEMBER Knmc Havn A porENTrALLy Drsquar,rrvmc
EcoNonrrc Ixrnnnsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect' distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
invesrment of $2,000 or more (section sito:(a); reg. lg703.l(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,-or holds any-position of
managemenr (section 87103(d); reg. 1 g703.1(b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 97r03(b); reg. rg703.z);
o An economic interest in any source of income, incruding promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to thJdecision (section g7103(c);
reg. 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section g7103(J); ,"glTa,7'u.+l:
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "per.sonal financial effects" rule
(section 87103; reg. 18703.5).
Section 82033 defines real property as: ..... any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an inteiest in real propefty located in the
jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public ofri.iut ...,, Because
Councilmember Kring has entered into a 1O-year lease of the property in the Garden
Walk, and that lease is p.resumably valued at $2,000 or more, she has an economic
interest in real property.l
Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operared for profit, including but nor limited to a propiietoiship, partnership, fir-,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association.,'
In order for a business entity to be considered an economic interest, an official
must have an investment of $2,000 or more or be a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or hold any position of management. In our previous letter, (White Advice
Letter, No. 4-07-035) we concluded that under the facts presented at that time,
"Councilmember Kring is not clllTently operating, nor does she have any investment in or
hold any position with, the wine bar." Accordinlly, we found that..Councilmember
Kring [did] not have an economic interest in a business entity.,,
Since that time you have now indicated that Councilmember Kring has
incorporated and is an officer of the corporation. Furthermore, she has leased a location
to operate her business and has hired an architect to design the space. Accordingly, she
has a position in the corporation and, presumably, an inv"estment of more than g2,000.
However, because the definition of business entity states that the organization must be
"operated" for profit, and you have indicated that the business has y-et to open, the

its purposes." For that.reasol we interpret the phrase "any organ izationor enterprise

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 1 8700(a).)

"(a) Directly involved real property.

governmental decision on the real property in which an official has a
leasehold interest is presumed to be material. This presumption may be
rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that thl governmental
decision will have any effect on any of the following:

(C) The value of the lessee's right to sublease the real property, either
positively or negatively;
(D) The legally allowable use or the current use of the real property
by the lessee; or
(E) The use or enjoyment of the leased real property by the lessee.

FACTS The City of Anaheim (the "City") is-a charter city consisting of approximately 50 square miles in northern orange county. The city has a current population of approximately 343,000. There are approximately 2,857 retail businesses licensed within the City of Anaheim. Councilmember Lucille Kring was first elected to the Anaheim City Council (the "Council") in November l99g and served one four-year term, which expired in November 2002. She was not a member of the Council between November 2OOZ and, November 2006. She was re-elected to the Council in Novemb er 2Oo6where she currently serves. The Anaheim Garden Walk (the "Garden Walk") Project is an approximately 29.1 acre mixed use project bordered by Katella Avenue, ruaibor Boulevard, tiirr"y way, and clementine Street.
